content.full_text:
SEP 17 '90 16:28 CARLSMITH BALL LONG

CALIFORNIA POLITICAL ATTORNEYS ASSOCIATION
301 East Ocean Boulevard, Suite 700
Long Beach, CA 90802-4828
Phone: (213) 435-5631
Fax: (213) 435-5631

September 17, 1990

Scott Hallabrin, Esquire
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: FPPC Wood Advice Letter I-90-388

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am writing on behalf of the California Political Attorneys Association concerning the above referenced advice letter. The advice letter (hereafter Wood letter) states that, "If a contribution of $25.00 or more is received from an individual for whom you do not have occupation and employer information, you must request the information from the contributor." (Emphasis added.) It is this conclusion which we wish reconsidered. For the reasons stated below we request that the Commission Staff reconsider and reverse this advice, or in the alternative, place the matter on the next available agenda for consideration by the Commission.

The only authority cited in the Wood letter is the Davidson Advice Letter, No. I-90-96. A careful reading of the Davidson letter does not support Staff's legal conclusion. At best, the Davidson letter "suggests" the information be requested. If the contribution is required to be disclosed on the campaign report, a statement should be included on the report to the effect the information was requested. (We note the Davidson letter fails to cite any authority for the suggestion offered.)

We have researched both statutes and regulations and find no authority for the requirement that committees request occupation and employer information for contributors over $25.00 and under $100.00. In fact, this point is conceded in the Davidson letter. That letter states in part, "You have asked what steps candidates and treasurers must take to obtain

Scott Hallabrin, Esquire  
September 17, 1990

occupation and employer information to comply with Section 84211. There is no regulation which pertains to this matter."

Government Code Section 84211 (cited above in the Davidson letter) merely requires committees to itemize contributors of $100.00 or more and include their occupation and employer. Section 84211 does not affirmatively require that committees obtain this information for contributions under $100.00. As is conceded in the Davidson letter, there is no regulation imposing this requirement.

The advice creates a new requirement that quite frankly will prove very burdensome to many committees, particularly those committees which rely on small contributions. Small contributions should be encouraged, not discouraged.

In addition, the Wood Advice Letter might have the unintended effect of encouraging committees to accept anonymous contributions under $100 which are legal per Government Code section 84304.

We respectfully request reconsideration and reversal of the advice requiring that committees request occupation and employer information of contributors who make contributions between $25.00 and $100.00 contained in the Wood Advice Letter.

Sincerely yours,

[Signature]
ALLAN E. TEBBETTS
Chairman, CPAA

AET:mm
